title_en: Waiting by a Tree for a Rabbit
pd_rationale: Originates in the Han Feizi (3rd c. BCE); classical Chinese text long in the public domain worldwide.
beats: A farmer sees a rabbit run into a tree stump and die.
He takes the rabbit home and eats well.
He abandons his farming to wait by the stump for more rabbits.
No more rabbits come and his field goes to ruin.
cultural_notes: Idiom warns against expecting windfalls without effort.

title_en: Pulling Up Sprouts to Help Them Grow
pd_rationale: From Mencius (4th c. BCE); classical text in the public domain worldwide.
beats: A farmer is impatient that his rice sprouts grow slowly.
He pulls each sprout upward to lengthen it.
He returns home exhausted and boasts of his help.
The next day every sprout has withered and died.
cultural_notes: Idiom warns against forcing progress against natural pace.

title_en: Drawing a Snake and Adding Feet
pd_rationale: From the Zhan Guo Ce (Warring States, compiled Han); classical text, public domain.
beats: Several men compete to draw a snake fastest to win a jug of wine.
The quickest finishes first but adds feet to show off.
Another finishes and points out snakes have no feet.
The show-off loses the wine.
cultural_notes: Idiom warns against ruining a good thing with needless additions.

pd_rationale: Ancient Chinese mythology attested in Han-era texts; traditional plot in the public domain.
beats: Hou Yi obtains an elixir of immortality.
Chang'e swallows the elixir to keep it from a thief.
She rises to the moon and lives there.
Hou Yi lays out her favorite foods to honor her.
cultural_notes: Associated with the Mid-Autumn Festival.

pd_rationale: Ancient Chinese mythology (Huainanzi and earlier); traditional plot in the public domain.
beats: Ten suns rise together and scorch the earth.
Crops die and people suffer from the heat.
The archer Hou Yi shoots down nine of the suns.
One sun remains and the world is saved.
cultural_notes: Explains why there is a single sun.

pd_rationale: Historical anecdote from the History of Song; classical record in the public domain.
beats: Children play beside a large water vat.
One child falls in and is drowning.
Young Sima Guang smashes the vat with a rock.
The water flows out and the child is saved.
cultural_notes: Celebrates quick, unconventional thinking.

pd_rationale: Historical anecdote from the Records of the Three Kingdoms; classical record, public domain.
beats: A huge elephant arrives and everyone wonders its weight.
No scale is large enough to weigh it.
Young Cao Chong loads the elephant onto a boat and marks the waterline.
He then loads stones to the same line and weighs the stones instead.
cultural_notes: Celebrates displacement reasoning.

pd_rationale: Aesop's fable (Perry Index 226), antiquity; public-domain plot and PD translations only.
beats: A hare mocks a slow tortoise.
They agree to a race.
The hare races ahead then naps, sure of winning.
The tortoise keeps going steadily and wins while the hare sleeps.
cultural_notes: Moral: steady persistence beats careless speed.

pd_rationale: Aesop's fable (Perry Index 210), antiquity; public-domain plot and PD translations only.
beats: A shepherd boy lies that a wolf is coming to draw the villagers.
The villagers rush up and find no wolf; he laughs.
He repeats the lie and is trusted less.
When a real wolf comes no one believes him.
cultural_notes: Moral: liars are not believed even when truthful.

title_en: The North Wind and the Sun
pd_rationale: Aesop's fable (Perry Index 46), antiquity; public-domain plot and PD translations only.
beats: The North Wind and the Sun argue who is stronger.
They test their strength on a traveler's coat.
The Wind blows hard but the traveler clutches the coat tighter.
The Sun shines warmly and the traveler removes the coat; the Sun wins.
cultural_notes: Moral: gentleness succeeds where force fails.

title_en: Covering One's Ears While Stealing a Bell
pd_rationale: From the Lüshi Chunqiu (3rd c. BCE); classical Chinese text long in the public domain worldwide.
beats: A thief wants to steal a large bell but cannot carry it.
He decides to smash it into pieces to transport it.
Afraid the noise will alert others, he covers his own ears while smashing.
He is caught, having fooled only himself.
cultural_notes: Idiom condemns self-deception and ignoring obvious facts.

title_en: Mending the Pen After Losing Sheep
pd_rationale: From the Zhan Guo Ce (Warring States period); classical text, public domain worldwide.
beats: A farmer's sheep pen has a gap in the fence.
A wolf enters at night and takes a sheep.
Neighbors urge the farmer to repair the fence but he delays.
More sheep are taken before he finally fixes the pen.
cultural_notes: Idiom means it is never too late to correct a mistake.

title_en: The Frog at the Bottom of the Well
pd_rationale: From the Zhuangzi (4th c. BCE); classical Daoist text in the public domain worldwide.
beats: A frog lives contentedly at the bottom of a shallow well.
A sea turtle visits and describes the vastness of the ocean.
The frog is stunned to learn how small his world truly is.
The frog realizes his perspective was narrow and limited.
cultural_notes: Idiom describes a person with a narrow outlook.

title_en: Marking the Boat to Find a Lost Sword
pd_rationale: From the Lüshi Chunqiu (3rd c. BCE); classical Chinese text in the public domain worldwide.
beats: A man on a moving boat drops his sword into the river.
He calmly carves a mark on the side of the boat.
When the boat reaches shore, he dives at the mark to retrieve the sword.
The sword is long gone; the boat has moved far downstream.
cultural_notes: Idiom mocks rigid thinking that ignores changing circumstances.

pd_rationale: From the Xin Xu (Han dynasty, 1st c. BCE); classical text, public domain worldwide.
beats: Lord Ye fills his home with dragon carvings and paintings.
A real dragon in heaven hears of Lord Ye's devotion.
The dragon descends to visit, peering through the window.
Lord Ye flees in terror, revealing his love was only superficial.
cultural_notes: Idiom describes someone who professes love for something they actually fear.

title_en: The Fox Borrows the Tiger's Might
pd_rationale: From the Zhan Guo Ce (Warring States period); classical text in the public domain worldwide.
beats: A tiger catches a fox and is about to eat it.
The fox claims to be the king of beasts, sent by heaven.
The fox walks ahead with the tiger following; all animals flee in terror.
The tiger thinks they fear the fox, not realizing they fear the tiger behind it.
cultural_notes: Idiom describes someone using a powerful patron to intimidate others.

title_en: The Old Man at the Frontier Lost His Horse
pd_rationale: From the Huainanzi (2nd c. BCE); classical Chinese text in the public domain worldwide.
beats: An old man's horse runs away; neighbors come to console him.
He says it may be a blessing. The horse returns with several wild horses.
His son breaks a leg riding; the old man says it may be a blessing.
War comes and the son avoids conscription because of the injury.
cultural_notes: Idiom teaches that fortune and misfortune are intertwined and unpredictable.

title_en: Mistaking a Bow's Reflection for a Snake
pd_rationale: From the Jin Shu (7th c. CE) and earlier anecdotes; classical Chinese text in the public domain.
beats: A man sees what appears to be a snake swimming in his wine cup.
He drinks it anyway and becomes severely ill from fear.
His friend discovers the snake was the reflection of a bow hanging on the wall.
The man's illness vanishes instantly upon learning the truth.
cultural_notes: Idiom warns against groundless fears and self-inflicted anxiety.

title_en: Playing the Lute to a Cow
pd_rationale: From the Mouzi Lihuo Lun (Han dynasty, 2nd c. CE); classical Buddhist-apologetic text, public domain.
beats: A musician plays an elegant melody before a cow.
The cow continues eating grass, showing no reaction.
The musician changes to sounds imitating insects and a calf.
The cow pricks up its ears, understanding only sounds relevant to it.
cultural_notes: Idiom means speaking to the wrong audience or reasoning with an uncomprehending listener.

title_en: Adding Pupils to a Painted Dragon
pd_rationale: Tang dynasty anecdote about the painter Zhang Sengyao; classical record in the public domain.
beats: A famous painter paints four dragons on a temple wall without pupils.
He explains that adding pupils would make them fly away.
People insist and he dots the pupils of two dragons.
Thunder roars and the two dragons come alive, flying into the sky.
cultural_notes: Idiom means adding the finishing touch that brings a work to life.

title_en: The Man of Qi Who Worried About the Sky
pd_rationale: From the Liezi (4th c. BCE); classical Daoist text in the public domain worldwide.
beats: A man from the state of Qi constantly fears the sky will fall down.
He cannot eat or sleep because of his anxiety.
A friend explains the sky is just accumulated air and cannot fall.
The man finally understands and stops worrying.

characters.gloss: man from the state of Qi

cultural_notes: Idiom mocks unnecessary and groundless worrying.

pd_rationale: From the Han Feizi (3rd c. BCE); classical Legalist text in the public domain worldwide.
beats: A merchant boasts his shields can block any weapon.
He also claims his spears can pierce any shield.
A bystander asks what happens when his spear strikes his shield.
The merchant is speechless and cannot answer.
cultural_notes: Idiom describes a logical contradiction or inconsistency.

pd_rationale: From the Zhuangzi (4th c. BCE); classical Daoist text in the public domain worldwide.
beats: The beauty Xi Shi frowns due to chest pain and still looks lovely.
An ugly woman named Dong Shi tries to copy the frown.
Dong Shi's imitated expression makes her look even more grotesque.
People flee at the sight of her, and she is ridiculed.
cultural_notes: Idiom warns against blind imitation without understanding.

title_en: Three in the Morning, Four in the Evening
pd_rationale: From the Zhuangzi (4th c. BCE); classical Daoist text in the public domain worldwide.
beats: A monkey keeper tells his monkeys they will get three nuts in the morning and four at night.
The monkeys are furious at the morning ration.
He changes the plan to four in the morning and three at night.
The monkeys are delighted, not realizing the total is unchanged.
cultural_notes: Idiom describes someone who is fickle or changes their mind frequently; also a lesson about being fooled by appearances.

pd_rationale: From the Zhan Guo Ce (Warring States period); classical text in the public domain worldwide.
beats: A man wants to travel south to the state of Chu.
He drives his carriage north and insists he will still reach Chu.
Passersby tell him he is heading the wrong way.
The further he travels, the further from Chu he gets.
cultural_notes: Idiom means acting contrary to one's stated goal.

title_en: The Snipe and the Clam Fight
pd_rationale: From the Zhan Guo Ce (Warring States period); classical text in the public domain worldwide.
beats: A clam opens its shell to bask in the sun on a riverbank.
A snipe pecks at the clam's flesh; the clam snaps shut on the snipe's beak.
Neither will let go, locked in a stalemate.
A fisherman comes along and catches them both easily.
cultural_notes: Idiom warns that quarrels between two parties can allow a third party to benefit.

title_en: The Man from Zheng Buys Shoes
pd_rationale: From the Han Feizi (3rd c. BCE); classical Legalist text in the public domain worldwide.
beats: A man from Zheng measures his foot for new shoes and writes down the size.
At the market, he realizes he left the measurement at home.
He rushes home to get the paper instead of trying the shoes on.
By the time he returns, the market has closed.

characters.gloss: man from the state of Zheng

cultural_notes: Idiom mocks rigid adherence to rules over practical sense.

title_en: The Cowherd and the Weaver Girl
pd_rationale: Ancient Chinese folk myth dating to the Han dynasty; traditional story in the public domain worldwide.
beats: A cowherd falls in love with Zhinü, a heavenly weaver fairy.
They marry but the Queen Mother of the West separates them with the Milky Way.
Magpies form a bridge across the Milky Way once a year.
They reunite on the seventh day of the seventh lunar month.
cultural_notes: Associated with the Qixi Festival (Chinese Valentine's Day).

pd_rationale: Ancient Chinese creation myth recorded in texts such as Huainanzi and Liezi; traditional story in the public domain.
beats: A great battle breaks one of the pillars holding up the sky, causing chaos.
Fires, floods, and wild beasts torment humanity.
The goddess Nüwa melts five-colored stones to patch the holes in the sky.
She severs a giant turtle's legs to support the four corners of heaven.
cultural_notes: Explains the origin of the sky's colors and earth's stability.

pd_rationale: Ancient Chinese creation myth recorded in texts from the Three Kingdoms period; traditional story in the public domain.
beats: In the beginning, the universe is a cosmic egg containing chaos.
Pangu sleeps inside for 18,000 years and then awakens.
He separates heaven and earth with his axe and holds them apart.
Upon his death, his body becomes mountains, rivers, the sun, the moon, and all life.
cultural_notes: Foundational Chinese creation myth explaining the origin of the world.

pd_rationale: From the Shan Hai Jing (Classic of Mountains and Seas); ancient text in the public domain worldwide.
beats: The giant Kuafu decides to chase and catch the sun.
He runs across the land but grows desperately thirsty.
He drinks the Yellow River and Wei River dry but it is not enough.
He dies of thirst and his staff transforms into a peach forest.
cultural_notes: Symbolizes heroic ambition, perseverance, and the value of striving.

title_en: Yu the Great Controls the Floods
pd_rationale: Ancient Chinese legend recorded in the Shiji and other classical texts; traditional story in the public domain.
beats: Ancient China suffers from devastating floods; Gun tries to dam them but fails.
His son Yu changes strategy, digging channels to guide water to the sea.
Yu works for 13 years, passing his own home three times without entering.
He succeeds in controlling the floods and becomes the founder of the Xia dynasty.
cultural_notes: Represents selfless dedication to the greater good.

pd_rationale: Ancient Chinese legend dating to the Eastern Jin dynasty; traditional story in the public domain worldwide.
beats: Zhu Yingtai disguises herself as a boy to attend school.
She becomes close friends with classmate Liang Shanbo and falls in love.
Liang learns her true identity too late; her family has already arranged her marriage.
Liang dies of grief; at his grave, Zhu leaps in and both souls transform into butterflies.
cultural_notes: Known as the Chinese Romeo and Juliet; symbol of eternal love.

title_en: Meng Jiangnü Weeps at the Great Wall
pd_rationale: Ancient Chinese legend dating to the Spring and Autumn period; traditional story in the public domain.
beats: Meng Jiangnü's husband is conscripted to build the Great Wall.
She journeys far to bring him winter clothing.
She learns he has died and his body is buried within the wall.
Her grieving is so intense that a section of the wall collapses, revealing his remains.
cultural_notes: One of China's Four Great Folktales; symbol of wifely devotion and tragedy of forced labor.

title_en: The Eight Immortals Cross the Sea
pd_rationale: Traditional Taoist legend dating to the Tang/Song dynasties; folklore in the public domain worldwide.
beats: Eight immortals need to cross the sea to attend a celestial banquet.
Each immortal uses their unique magical item as a vessel to cross.
A dragon prince steals one immortal, sparking a battle.
The immortals unite their powers, defeat the dragon, and continue on.
cultural_notes: Proverbial expression meaning each person shows their unique skill.

pd_rationale: Mythological story from the Fengshen Yanyi (16th c. CE); classical Chinese novel in the public domain.
beats: Nezha is born as a miraculous child with supernatural powers.
He fights and slays a dragon king's son who had been terrorizing villagers.
The dragon kings demand his life in revenge.
Nezha sacrifices himself but is reborn from a lotus flower.
cultural_notes: Popular protective deity figure in Chinese folk religion; symbolizes rebellion against tyranny.

pd_rationale: Traditional Chinese folk legend dating to the Tang/Song dynasties; folklore in the public domain worldwide.
beats: A white snake spirit transforms into a beautiful woman named Bai Suzhen.
She falls in love with and marries the scholar Xu Xian.
A Buddhist monk named Fahai exposes her true identity.
Fahai imprisons Bai Suzhen under the Leifeng Pagoda.

characters.gloss: Bai Suzhen, the white snake spirit

cultural_notes: One of China's Four Great Folktales; explores themes of love transcending nature.

pd_rationale: From the Shan Hai Jing (Classic of Mountains and Seas); ancient text in the public domain worldwide.
beats: The daughter of Emperor Yan drowns while playing in the Eastern Sea.
Her spirit transforms into the Jingwei bird.
The bird carries twigs and pebbles from the mountains to drop into the sea.
Vowing to fill the sea so no one else will drown, she persists endlessly.
cultural_notes: Symbol of dogged determination against impossible odds.

pd_rationale: Traditional Chinese legend dating to the Tang dynasty; folklore in the public domain worldwide.
beats: A scholar named Zhong Kui takes the imperial exam but is cheated of first place.
In shame, he kills himself but the emperor honors him posthumously.
His spirit becomes the king of ghosts, tasked with subduing evil spirits.
His image is hung on doors to ward off demons.
cultural_notes: Protective folk deity; his image is a traditional New Year talisman against evil.

title_en: The Foolish Old Man Removes the Mountains
pd_rationale: From the Liezi (4th c. BCE); classical Daoist text in the public domain worldwide.
beats: Two great mountains block the entrance to Old Fool's home.
He decides to dig them away with his family using only hoes.
A wise man mocks him, but Old Fool says his descendants will continue the work.
The gods are moved by his determination and move the mountains for him.
cultural_notes: Teaches that perseverance can overcome any obstacle.

title_en: The Blind Men and the Elephant
pd_rationale: Originally from the Mahaparinirvana Sutra (Buddhist scripture, ancient India); translated into Chinese, public domain.
beats: Several blind men each touch a different part of an elephant.
One feels the trunk and says it is like a snake.
Another feels the leg and says it is like a tree trunk.
They argue fiercely, each convinced only their description is correct.
cultural_notes: Teaches the danger of drawing conclusions from limited experience.

title_en: An Old Horse Knows the Way
pd_rationale: From the Han Feizi (3rd c. BCE); classical Legalist text in the public domain worldwide.
beats: An army returning from a campaign gets lost in unfamiliar terrain.
The minister Guan Zhong suggests releasing the old warhorses.
The old horses find the path back to the camp on their own.
The army follows the horses and returns safely.
cultural_notes: Idiom means experience brings wisdom; old hands know the ropes.

title_en: A Mantis Tries to Stop a Carriage
pd_rationale: From the Zhuangzi (4th c. BCE); classical Daoist text in the public domain worldwide.
beats: A praying mantis stands on a road, raising its forelegs defiantly.
A heavy carriage approaches but the mantis refuses to move aside.
The mantis is crushed under the wheels.
The story warns against overestimating one's limited strength.
cultural_notes: Idiom warns against arrogantly opposing overwhelming force.

title_en: The Donkey of Guizhou Has Exhausted Its Tricks
pd_rationale: Fable by Liu Zongyuan (Tang dynasty, 9th c. CE); classical Chinese literary work in the public domain.
beats: A tiger in Guizhou encounters a donkey for the first time and is frightened.
The donkey brays loudly; the tiger retreats in alarm.
After observing the donkey over time, the tiger realizes it can only bray and kick.
The tiger attacks and devours the donkey.
cultural_notes: Idiom means someone has exhausted all their tricks and is now powerless.

pd_rationale: From the Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian, 1st c. BCE); classical historical text in the public domain.
beats: General Lian Po resents minister Lin Xiangru for outranking him.
Lin avoids confrontation publicly to preserve unity for the state.
Lian Po realizes his pettiness and strips to his bare shoulders.
He carries thorny branches on his back and kneels before Lin to apologize.
cultural_notes: Model for genuine repentance and putting state interests above personal pride.

title_en: Returning the Jade Intact to Zhao
pd_rationale: From the Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian, 1st c. BCE); classical historical text in the public domain.
beats: The King of Qin offers 15 cities to Zhao for their priceless jade disk.
Minister Lin Xiangru is sent to deliver it, suspecting a trick.
The Qin king handles the jade but shows no intention of giving cities.
Lin threatens to smash the jade and secretly returns it intact to Zhao.

characters.gloss: Lin Xiangru, a minister of Zhao

cultural_notes: Idiom means returning something to its owner in perfect condition.

pd_rationale: From the Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian, 1st c. BCE); classical historical text in the public domain.
beats: Chancellor Zhao Gao presents a deer to the young emperor and calls it a horse.
The emperor says it is clearly a deer.
Zhao Gao asks the ministers for their opinion to test their loyalty.
Those who contradict Zhao Gao are later executed; those who agree survive.
cultural_notes: Idiom describes deliberately distorting the truth and abusing power.

pd_rationale: From the Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian, 1st c. BCE); classical historical text in the public domain.
beats: Zhao Kuo is a brilliant military theorist who can recite every strategy.
He is appointed general despite having no actual combat experience.
He ignores the advice of seasoned officers and changes battle plans on theory alone.
His army is annihilated at Changping and he is killed in battle.
cultural_notes: Idiom criticizes empty theorizing without practical experience.

title_en: Sleeping on Brushwood and Tasting Gall
pd_rationale: From the Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian, 1st c. BCE); classical historical text in the public domain.
beats: King Goujian of Yue is defeated and forced to serve as the enemy king's servant.
After being released, he sleeps on brushwood and tastes bitter gall daily.
He endures hardship to remember his humiliation and rebuild his strength.
After years of preparation, he defeats his enemy and reclaims his kingdom.
cultural_notes: Idiom celebrates enduring hardship to achieve a great goal.

title_en: Three Visits to the Thatched Hut
pd_rationale: From the Records of the Three Kingdoms (3rd c. CE); classical historical text in the public domain worldwide.
beats: Warlord Liu Bei seeks to recruit the brilliant strategist Zhuge Liang.
Twice he travels to Zhuge's thatched hut but misses him.
On the third visit, Zhuge Liang is home and impressed by Liu Bei's sincerity.
Zhuge Liang agrees to serve and helps establish the Shu Han kingdom.
cultural_notes: Symbol of sincere effort to recruit talent.

title_en: Quenching Thirst by Gazing at Plums
pd_rationale: From the Shishuo Xinyu (5th c. CE); classical anecdote collection in the public domain worldwide.
beats: Cao Cao's army is marching under scorching heat with no water source.
Soldiers are collapsing from thirst and morale is plummeting.
Cao Cao announces there is a plum forest ahead with sour fruit.
The mere thought makes the soldiers salivate and they find strength to continue.
cultural_notes: Idiom means using wishful thinking to comfort oneself.

title_en: Breaking the Cauldrons and Sinking the Boats
pd_rationale: From the Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian, 1st c. BCE); classical historical text in the public domain.
beats: Xiang Yu leads his army across a river to fight a much larger Qin force.
After crossing, he orders all cooking cauldrons smashed and boats sunk.
His army has no way to retreat and must win or die.
They fight with desperate courage and win a decisive victory.
cultural_notes: Idiom means to burn bridges and commit absolutely with no retreat.

pd_rationale: From the Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian, 1st c. BCE); classical historical text in the public domain.
beats: Xiang Yu's army is surrounded by Liu Bang's forces.
At night, Liu Bang's soldiers sing folk songs from the Chu region.
Xiang Yu's soldiers hear the songs and believe their homeland has fallen.
Morale collapses, many desert, and Xiang Yu faces his final defeat.
cultural_notes: Idiom means being surrounded by enemies on all sides with no escape.

title_en: Kong Rong Gives Away the Bigger Pears
pd_rationale: Historical anecdote from the Later Han; recorded in classical texts in the public domain worldwide.
beats: Young Kong Rong is offered a plate of pears with his brothers.
He deliberately chooses the smallest pear.
When asked why, he says he is the youngest and should defer to his elders.
His humility at age four becomes a model of filial virtue for generations.
cultural_notes: Classic lesson in humility, filial piety, and consideration for others.

title_en: Amaze the World with a Single Cry
pd_rationale: From the Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian, 1st c. BCE); classical historical text in the public domain.
beats: King Zhuang of Chu neglects state affairs for three years, spending time in pleasure.
A minister tells him a riddle about a bird that never sings.
The king replies that the bird will soar and amaze all when it chooses to cry.
King Zhuang reforms, becomes a great ruler, and makes Chu a dominant power.
cultural_notes: Idiom describes someone who surprises others with a brilliant achievement after obscurity.

title_en: Tying Hair to a Beam and Stabbing a Thigh
pd_rationale: Historical anecdotes from the Zhan Guo Ce and Han dynasty records; classical texts in the public domain worldwide.
beats: Sun Jing ties his hair to a roof beam so that if he dozes off while studying, the jerk wakes him.
Su Qin pricks his own thigh with an awl whenever he feels drowsy.
Both men endure pain to stay awake and continue studying.
Their extreme dedication leads them to become great scholars and statesmen.
cultural_notes: Idiom describes extreme dedication to study and self-discipline.

pd_rationale: Buddhist parable recorded in the Mahāsāṃghika Vinaya (《摩诃僧祇律》, 5th c. translation); classical text in the public domain.
beats: A group of monkeys sees the moon's reflection in a well and thinks the moon has fallen in.
They fear the world will be dark and decide to rescue it.
They form a chain hanging from a tree branch down to the water.
The branch breaks and they tumble into the water; looking up, they see the moon still in the sky.
cultural_notes: Idiom 猴子捞月 warns against wasted effort chasing an illusion.

pd_rationale: Aesop's fable (Perry Index 390), antiquity; public-domain plot and PD translations only.
beats: A thirsty crow finds a pitcher with a small amount of water at the bottom.
The water level is too low for the crow to reach with its beak.
The crow drops pebbles into the pitcher one by one.
The water level rises and the crow drinks.
cultural_notes: Moral: clever thinking and persistence can solve difficult problems.

pd_rationale: Traditional Chinese folk tale and New Year woodblock-print subject; long in the public domain.
beats: A mouse father wants the most powerful husband in the world for his daughter.
He asks the sun, but the cloud can block the sun; he asks the cloud, but the wind blows it away.
He asks the wind, but a wall stops it; he asks the wall, but a mouse can gnaw through it.
He realizes a mouse is best and marries his daughter to a fine young mouse.
cultural_notes: A New Year folk tale; the circular search shows everyone has a place.

title_en: The Parrot Tries to Put Out the Fire
pd_rationale: Buddhist fable from the Liu Song-era 《宣验记》; classical text in the public domain.
beats: A parrot lives happily on a mountain full of birds and beasts.
The parrot flies away, but later sees the mountain caught in a great fire.
It dips its wings in water and sprinkles drops on the flames, again and again.
A heavenly god, moved by its sincerity though the effort is tiny, sends rain to put out the fire.
cultural_notes: Celebrates sincere effort and loyalty to one's home, however small the deed.

pd_rationale: Aesop's fable (Perry Index 124), antiquity; public-domain plot and PD translations only.
beats: A crow sits on a branch with a piece of cheese in its beak.
A fox approaches and praises the crow, calling it the king of birds.
The fox asks the crow to sing so it can prove its beautiful voice.
The crow opens its beak to sing, dropping the cheese for the fox to snatch.
cultural_notes: Moral: beware of flattery; it often conceals a selfish motive.

pd_rationale: From the Han-dynasty 《说苑》 by Liu Xiang; classical text in the public domain.
beats: A cicada drinks dew high on a tree, unaware of danger.
A mantis behind it raises its arms to catch the cicada, unaware of danger.
A sparrow behind the mantis stretches to eat it, unaware of the boy with a slingshot below.
Each pursues its gain blind to the peril at its own back.
cultural_notes: Idiom 螳螂捕蝉，黄雀在后 warns against greed that ignores hidden danger.

pd_rationale: From the pre-Qin 《韩非子》; classical text in the public domain.
beats: Zengzi's wife, going to market, tells her crying son she will kill a pig for him if he stays.
When she returns, she finds Zengzi about to slaughter the family pig.
She protests that she was only joking to quiet the child.
Zengzi kills the pig, saying parents must never deceive children, for they learn by example.
cultural_notes: A classic lesson on honesty and keeping promises to children.

pd_rationale: Aesop's fable (Perry Index 176), antiquity; public-domain plot and PD translations only.
beats: A farmer finds a snake frozen in the winter snow.
He takes pity on the snake and warms it against his chest.
The snake revives and immediately bites the farmer.
The dying farmer realizes that evil nature cannot be changed by kindness.
cultural_notes: Moral: some people are inherently malicious; kindness will not change them.

pd_rationale: From the Ming-dynasty tale 《中山狼传》; classical text in the public domain.
beats: A hunted wolf begs the scholar Mr. Dongguo to hide it in his book bag.
Mr. Dongguo pities the wolf and conceals it, misleading the hunter.
Once safe, the ungrateful wolf wants to eat its rescuer.
A passing farmer tricks the wolf back into the bag and kills it, saving the foolish scholar.
cultural_notes: Idiom 东郭先生 mocks misplaced pity toward the wicked.

title_en: Bend the Chimney, Move the Firewood
pd_rationale: From the Han-dynasty 《汉书·霍光传》; classical text in the public domain.
beats: A guest sees a straight chimney beside a stack of firewood and warns the host of fire.
He advises bending the chimney and moving the firewood away; the host ignores him.
A fire breaks out, and neighbors who help fight it are thanked and feasted.
Someone notes the guest whose warning would have prevented it all was never even invited.
cultural_notes: Idiom 曲突徙薪 praises prevention over praising those who clean up disasters.

title_en: Ma Liang and the Magic Brush
pd_rationale: Chinese folk tale written by Hong Xuntao, mid-20th century; widely circulated as public domain children's literature in China.
beats: A poor boy named Ma Liang loves to draw but cannot afford a brush.
In a dream, an old man gives him a magic brush that brings drawings to life.
He uses it to help the poor but refuses to serve a greedy official.
He draws a door to escape and continues using his brush for good.

characters.gloss: Ma Liang, a boy with a magic brush

cultural_notes: Beloved classic of Chinese children's literature; celebrates creativity and justice.

pd_rationale: Traditional Chinese folk tale with variants across East Asia; ancient folklore in the public domain worldwide.
beats: A poor farmer finds a large snail and places it in his water jar.
Each day he returns from the fields to find a hot meal mysteriously prepared.
He hides and discovers the snail transforms into a beautiful maiden.
They marry and live happily together.
cultural_notes: Representative Chinese folk tale about kindness being rewarded.

pd_rationale: Nasreddin Hodja folk tales from Turkic/Uyghur tradition, transmitted orally for centuries; traditional folklore in the public domain.
beats: Afanti is a witty sage who travels on his donkey across the land.
He uses clever wordplay and reverse logic to solve disputes.
Greedy kings and officials try to trap him with impossible demands.
Each time Afanti outsmarts them, often turning their logic against them.

characters.gloss: Afanti (Nasreddin), a wise and witty man

cultural_notes: Beloved folk figure across Central Asia and western China; symbol of folk wisdom.

pd_rationale: Traditional Mongolian/Chinese folk tale transmitted orally; public domain folklore.
beats: The hunter Hailibu saves a small white snake from a predator.
The snake's father, the Dragon King, gives Hailibu a gem to understand animal speech.
He overhears animals discussing an impending flood but cannot reveal the secret or he will turn to stone.
He chooses to warn the villagers and is petrified as a result.
cultural_notes: Teaches the value of self-sacrifice for the greater good.

pd_rationale: Traditional Chinese folklore dating to the Han dynasty; cultural tradition in the public domain worldwide.
beats: The Jade Emperor holds a race across a river to select the zodiac animals.
The rat tricks the cat and rides on the ox's back to win first place.
Each animal uses its unique skills to cross the river.
The order is set: Rat, Ox, Tiger, Rabbit, Dragon, Snake, Horse, Goat, Monkey, Rooster, Dog, Pig.
cultural_notes: Explains the origin of the Chinese zodiac and why the cat is not included.

pd_rationale: Traditional Chinese folk legend dating to the Tang dynasty; folklore in the public domain worldwide.
beats: A goddess descends to earth and marries a mortal, bearing a son named Chen Xiang.
Her brother, a god, imprisons her under Mount Hua for breaking celestial law.
Chen Xiang obtains the magical Lotus Lantern and trains to confront the gods.
He splits the mountain with a divine axe and rescues his mother.
cultural_notes: Tale of filial devotion overcoming even divine authority.

pd_rationale: Traditional Chinese folklore based on the historical monk Daoji (12th c. CE); folk legend in the public domain.
beats: Ji Gong is a Buddhist monk with a dirty, ragged appearance and eccentric behavior.
He breaks monastic rules openly, eating meat and drinking wine.
Despite appearances, he helps the poor, heals the sick, and battles injustice.
His catchphrase is, 'Wine and meat pass through the body; the Buddha stays in the heart.'
cultural_notes: Beloved folk hero embodying the idea that true virtue transcends appearances.

pd_rationale: From the Lienü Zhuan (Han dynasty, 1st c. BCE); classical text in the public domain worldwide.
beats: Mencius's mother lives near a cemetery; young Mencius imitates funeral rites.
She moves near a marketplace; Mencius starts imitating merchants and butchers.
She moves near a school; Mencius begins imitating scholars and studying.
She stays there, and Mencius grows up to become a great Confucian philosopher.
cultural_notes: Classic lesson about the importance of environment in a child's upbringing.

title_en: The Legend of the Nian Beast
pd_rationale: Traditional Chinese folklore explaining Spring Festival customs; public domain folklore.
beats: A terrifying monster named Nian emerges each New Year's Eve to devour villagers and livestock.
Every year, villagers flee to the mountains to survive.
An old man discovers that Nian fears the color red, bright lights, and loud noises.
People use red couplets, lanterns, and firecrackers to drive Nian away; these become Spring Festival traditions.
cultural_notes: Explains the origins of Chinese New Year customs including firecrackers and red decorations.

title_en: The Tiger Learns from the Cat
pd_rationale: Traditional Chinese folk tale transmitted orally for centuries; public domain folklore.
beats: A tiger asks a cat to teach it all the skills of hunting.
The cat teaches the tiger everything except how to climb trees.
One day, the tiger turns on the cat, trying to eat its master.
The cat escapes by climbing a tree; the tiger never learns to climb.
cultural_notes: Folk tale about keeping a secret advantage and the danger of teaching everything to the ungrateful.

pd_rationale: Based on the anonymous Northern-dynasties 《木兰辞》(Ballad of Mulan); classical text in the public domain.
beats: The army conscripts one man from every family, but Mulan's father is old and her brother small.
Mulan buys a horse and armor and rides to war disguised as a man in her father's place.
She fights bravely for many years and earns great honor on the battlefield.
Offered high office, she asks only to return home, where her comrades learn she is a woman.

characters.gloss: Hua Mulan, a girl who takes her father's place in the army

cultural_notes: A celebrated tale of filial devotion and courage.

pd_rationale: From Wu Cheng'en's Ming-dynasty novel 《西游记》(Journey to the West); classical text in the public domain.
beats: The Monkey King Sun Wukong gains great powers and is given a minor post in Heaven.
Insulted by the trick, he rebels and declares himself Great Sage Equal to Heaven.
He battles the heavenly armies, eats the immortal peaches, and defeats every general.
Only the Buddha finally subdues him, trapping him beneath a mountain.
cultural_notes: One of the most beloved episodes of Chinese mythology.

pd_rationale: From Luo Guanzhong's Ming-dynasty novel 《三国演义》(Romance of the Three Kingdoms); classical text in the public domain.
beats: Zhou Yu, jealous of Zhuge Liang, orders him to produce 100,000 arrows in three days.
Zhuge Liang instead prepares twenty boats lined with straw bundles.
On a foggy night he sails them toward the enemy camp and beats war drums.
The enemy fires countless arrows into the straw; Zhuge Liang returns with more than enough.
cultural_notes: Idiom 草船借箭 celebrates wit that turns an enemy's strength to one's own use.

pd_rationale: From Shi Nai'an's Ming-dynasty novel 《水浒传》(Water Margin); classical text in the public domain.
beats: Wu Song drinks heavily at an inn and ignores warnings of a man-eating tiger on the ridge.
Climbing the hill at dusk, he suddenly meets the great tiger.
He dodges its lunges, and his wooden staff shatters against a tree.
With his bare hands he pins the tiger's head and beats it to death, saving the district.
cultural_notes: A famous display of courage and strength from the Water Margin.

title_en: Yue Fei's Mother Tattoos His Back
pd_rationale: Traditional legend of the Song-dynasty general Yue Fei; long in the public domain.
beats: Young Yue Fei wishes to serve his country as invaders threaten the Song.
His mother, to strengthen his resolve, tattoos four characters on his back.
The words read 尽忠报国 — 'serve the country with utter loyalty.'
Yue Fei grows into a great general, carrying his mother's charge all his life.
cultural_notes: A revered emblem of patriotism and a mother's teaching.
